flashcards.front: What is operator overloading in C++?
flashcards.back: A feature that allows redefining the behavior of existing operators (+, -, *, /, etc.) when applied to user-defined data types (classes). It makes code more intuitive and readable.

flashcards.front: What is the syntax for operator overloading?
flashcards.back: ReturnType operator<symbol>(parameters) { // body } - e.g., Complex operator+(const Complex& obj)

flashcards.front: Which operators cannot be overloaded in C++?
flashcards.back: :: (scope resolution), . (member access), .* (pointer-to-member), ?: (ternary), sizeof, typeid, and static_cast operators cannot be overloaded.

flashcards.front: Difference between unary and binary operator overloading?
flashcards.back: Unary operators work on one operand (++, --, -, !). Binary operators work on two operands (+, -, *, /, ==, etc.). Unary can be member or friend; binary typically uses two parameters.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between member function and friend function for operator overloading?
flashcards.back: Member function uses 'this' pointer (one less parameter), while friend function has no 'this' and requires explicit parameters for both operands.

flashcards.front: Why should operator+ return by value and not by reference?
flashcards.back: Returning by value creates a new object (rvalue). Returning reference would return a local/temporary object that gets destroyed, causing dangling reference.

flashcards.front: What is the rule for overloading && and || operators?
flashcards.back: C++ uses short-circuit evaluation. When overloaded, the operators lose short-circuit behavior and become regular function calls with different semantics.

flashcards.front: How to overload stream operators (<< and >>)?
flashcards.back: They must be overloaded as friend functions to allow object on right side: friend ostream& operator<<(ostream&, const ClassName&);

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of the subscript operator []?
flashcards.back: Provides array-like access to elements. Must be overloaded as non-const for modification and const for read-only access.
operator TypeName() { return value; } - Converts class object to specified type. Must be member function, no return type specified.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between ++prefix and postfix++ operators?
flashcards.back: Postfix takes dummy int parameter: operator++(int). Prefix returns reference to this, postfix returns by value (copy before increment).

flashcards.front: Why can't we overload operators for primitive types?
flashcards.back: Operators are already defined for built-in types. Overloading only works for user-defined types (classes/structs). It's a compile-time feature, not runtime.

flashcards.front: What is the rule for overloading assignment operator?
flashcards.back: Should return reference (*this) for chain assignment. Must handle self-assignment. Should be member function (not friend).
Number of operands - Unary operators: 1 operand, Binary operators: 2 operands. Cannot change arity of operators.

flashcards.front: Why use const with operator overloading?
flashcards.back: const ensures data integrity, enables const objects to use operators, and allows compiler optimizations. Should be used for read-only operations.
